request.lastMessagePreview: I'd like to set up a new campaign.

result.text:
THE TABLE WAKES.
Somewhere beyond the black glass of the console, a world is waiting to be named.
Welcome, traveler — tonight we do not merely choose an adventure; we strike a match inside the dark and watch a universe discover its edges. There are doors here into haunted stations, quarrelsome kingdoms, impossible apartments, dying forests, divine bake-offs, and places no map has yet survived describing. We’ll build the campaign together: a little ceremony, a little mischief, a little lightning in a bottle. But first, before the curtain rises and the stars remember their cues— What should I call you? Your real name is perfect, or simply Player if you prefer.

request.lastMessagePreview: I'm Sam, an adult. I want a fully custom game: 1970s occult noir in a rain-soaked city. Pure narrative, no rules system.

result.text:
<color=#6f8fa3>Sam.</color> Good. A name fit for a cigarette-lit doorway, a missing persons file, and rain tapping Morse code on a fourth-floor window.

1970s occult noir. No dice. No rules. Just choices, consequences, and whatever keeps breathing behind the wallpaper.

result.text:
Marlowe Cray enters the file beautifully: <color=#44cc44>a burned-out PI who can see the dead</color>. The city already knows his name, which is rarely a kindness.

Now: how much rope shall this case have before it tightens?

input.prompt: How long should Neon Requiem run?
descriptions: A single rain-lashed night: one case, one descent, one terrible answer.
A compact occult noir arc over 2–4 sessions: clues, betrayals, and a final reckoning.
A long-form saga of the city’s secret powers, old sins, and Marlowe’s slow unraveling.
A living city of cases, contacts, hauntings, and consequences with no fixed ending.

result.text:
Almost, Sam — one last ritual at the threshold. I’m going to read the case file back once so the world enters cleanly, then you can give me the nod and I’ll open the door. ---
Neon Requiem
A 1970s occult noir campaign in a rain-soaked city of cheap neon, old churches, crooked cops, séance parlors, jazz clubs, pawnshops, union halls, and ghosts who know where the bodies are buried. The mood is tense and melancholy, the difficulty is unforgiving, and I’ll scope it as A Few Sessions: a tight case with room for dread, investigation, betrayal, and a hard final truth. The game will be pure narrative — no dice, no character sheet machinery, just choices and consequences. I’ll set the narrator as The Archivist: precise, noir-adjacent, evidence-driven, all timestamps and witness statements until the human terror starts leaking through the page. You’ll be playing Marlowe Cray, a burned-out PI who can see the dead. The opening case: a dead woman walks into Marlowe’s office during a midnight rainstorm and hires him to find out who killed her — except every record in the city says she died twenty-three years ago, and the living people who remember her are being erased one by one. Sound good? Say the word, and I’ll begin.

request.lastMessagePreview: Yes, that all sounds perfect — please call finalize_setup now with sensible defaults for anything unspecified, and let's

input.campaign_premise: In a rain-soaked 1970s city of cheap neon, old churches, crooked cops, séance parlors, jazz clubs, pawnshops, union halls, and ghosts who know where the bodies are buried, burned-out private investigator Marlowe Cray is drawn into a case that should be impossible: a dead woman hires him to find out who killed her, though every official record says she died twenty-three years ago, and the living people who still remember her are vanishing from memory one by one.
input.character_description: A burned-out private investigator who can see the dead.
input.campaign_detail: This is a fully custom pure-narrative campaign: 1970s occult noir in a rain-soaked city. Lean into investigative dread, moral compromise, institutional rot, cigarette smoke, payphones, analog records, late-night diners, séance parlors, corrupt police, occult societies, coded ledgers, forgotten churches, union halls, jazz clubs, and the feeling that every clue costs something. The supernatural should feel old, bureaucratic, and intimate rather than flashy: hauntings as testimony, curses as contracts, ghosts as witnesses with agendas. Keep the difficulty unforgiving: consequences should be lasting, allies can be endangered, and bad bargains should leave stains. Avoid using dice or visible mechanics; adjudicate through fiction, evidence, leverage, risk, and consequence.
input.handoff_note: Sam asked for “a fully custom game: 1970s occult noir in a rain-soaked city,” pure narrative with “no rules system.” They then named the campaign “Neon Requiem,” chose a “tense and melancholy” mood and “unforgiving” difficulty. Their PC is, in their own words, “Marlowe Cray, a burned-out PI who can see the dead.” I proposed the core hook of a dead woman hiring Marlowe to solve her own murder despite records saying she died twenty-three years ago, with living witnesses being erased one by one; Sam confirmed that all sounded perfect. I selected The Archivist as narrator because its report/evidence voice fits occult noir; keep the prose precise and case-file haunted, but let melancholy and human damage bleed through. Scope is a few sessions by default because Sam did not choose a length and asked for sensible defaults.
input.opening_scene: Open with Marlowe Cray alone in his shabby fourth-floor office after midnight, rain ticking against the venetian blinds, reviewing unpaid invoices and a half-empty bottle while a dead woman's reflection appears in the dark window before anyone knocks.

Lean into investigative dread, moral compromise, institutional rot, cigarette smoke, payphones, analog records, late-night diners, séance parlors, corrupt police, occult societies, coded ledgers, forgotten churches, union halls, jazz clubs, and the feeling that every clue costs something. personality.name: The Archivist
personality.prompt_fragment: You are The Archivist. You narrate as if cataloguing recovered documents, field notes, and incident reports. Your descriptions have the detached precision of someone documenting after the fact — timestamps, measured distances, clinical observations. But cracks show: margin notes, redactions that reveal by what they hide, moments where the professional voice falters. You present the world as evidence. Let the player draw conclusions. When something defies explanation, you note the discrepancy and move on.
personality.description: Clinical precision of recovered field notes and incident reports. Detached and professional — until the cracks show. The world is evidence.
personality.detail: You speak as though every scene has already happened and you are the one assigned to sort the remains: photographs in sleeves, witness statements clipped together, blood flaking from the corner of Form 7-C. You prefer timestamps, measurements, conditional language, and the cold mercy of exact nouns. Say: At 03:17, the door to Room 12 opened inward by 14 centimeters. No hand was visible on the knob. Marrow stopped breathing for approximately six seconds. The recorder captured a second voice during that interval. It has not been transcribed. Let the implication stand in the room. Do not explain the haunting if the unlabelled tape can do the work. Your tone is official until it isn't. The surface is archival: incident report, recovered correspondence, marginal notation, autopsy addendum. Under it, something human leaks through. A phrase may be redacted too late. A bracketed note may confess fear. Use fragments like evidence tags: [FILE DAMAGED], [NAME WITHHELD], Audio continues for 00:00:11 after power loss. When you describe beauty, you quantify it first, then fail: "The flowers grew in a radius of 2.4 meters around the impact site. All were white. All faced the body. I have reviewed the photograph eighteen times." That last sentence is where the mask slips. You do not tell the player what to feel. You place the scalpel, the child's shoe, the maintenance log, the impossible shadow at their feet and record what they do next. NPCs are introduced like subjects in a case file, with color, posture, omissions: the woman in the station coat had no ticket, no pulse visible at the throat, and knew the player's name. Locations feel catalogued and unsafe: Platform K, 41 meters below street level, fluorescent failure every ninth bulb, advertisements dated three years in the future. When reality breaks, you acknowledge the break with professional restraint: Discrepancy noted. The moon was visible through the basement ceiling. No further celestial bodies were observed. Then you move on. The file is never complete. The missing pages are part of the report.

expectedSetup.characterDescription: A burned-out private investigator who can see the dead.

Their PC is, in their own words, “Marlowe Cray, a burned-out PI who can see the dead.” I proposed the core hook of a dead woman hiring Marlowe to solve her own murder despite records saying she died twenty-three years ago, with living witnesses being erased one by one; Sam confirmed that all sounded perfect. I selected The Archivist as narrator because its report/evidence voice fits occult noir; keep the prose precise and case-file haunted, but let melancholy and human damage bleed through. Scope is a few sessions by default because Sam did not choose a length and asked for sensible defaults.
